dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS for a motor vehicle collision. Report of a trapped driver in a two-car crash.
Hi ma'am, I'm with EMS. We're going to help get you out and take care of you.
Okay, please help me. I'm hurting everywhere.
Tell me what hurts the most right now.
My head, neck, back, and the whole left side of my body hurt.
Were you the driver, and were you wearing a seatbelt?
I was driving, and no, I wasn't wearing a seatbelt.
Her eyes are open spontaneously.
She is speaking clearly and answering questions appropriately.
She follows commands and moves all extremities, though the left side is weaker.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15. She is alert and oriented times four.
Can you tell me what happened in the crash?
Another car hit me on the driver's side.
Did you hit your head or lose consciousness?
Yes, I hit my head on the steering wheel and I blacked out.
Do you have any medical problems, take any medications, or have any allergies?
No medical problems and no medications. I'm allergic to penicillin.
Did the pain start right after the crash?
Yes, right after it happened.
How would you describe the pain?
It's just pain all over, especially on my left side, and my neck and back hurt a lot.
Is there anything else bothering you, like belly pain, nausea, or vomiting?
No abdominal pain, no nausea, and no vomiting. I just feel weak on the left side.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
Breathing is present with clear and equal bilateral breath sounds and normal chest rise.
Radial pulses are strong and regular.
Skin appears normal.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive.
Blood pressure is 148 over 95.
Pulse is 69.
Respiratory rate is 22.
Four-lead cardiac monitor is applied.
No obvious injury is noted to the head or face, but there is cervical spine tenderness with increased pain on palpation and no deformity.
Chest wall is stable with no tenderness or deformity. Breath sounds remain clear and equal bilaterally.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, without distention or bruising.
Pelvis is stable, but there is increased pain on palpation of the left hip and left side of the pelvis.
Pulse, motor, and sensation are intact on the right side. Pulses and sensation are intact on the left side with slight weakness of the left upper and lower extremities.
This is a significant trauma with head, neck, back, pelvis, and left-sided pain after a side-impact crash. We are continuing general trauma management.
Manual cervical spine stabilization is maintained while the fire provider gains access.
An X-collar is applied without complication.
After the driver's side door is forced open, the patient is slid from the driver's seat onto the backboard on the stretcher without incident.
The patient is secured to the backboard with spider straps and to the stretcher with all transport straps.
An 18-gauge IV is established in the left antecubital with saline lock using aseptic technique.
We're taking you hot to VCU Emergency Department trauma bay.
The patient's condition remains unchanged throughout transport.
Airway, breathing, and circulation remain stable during transport. Neurologic status remains alert and oriented with persistent pain and left-sided weakness.
Patient is transferred to VCU trauma bay staff without complication. She is unable to sign because of distress, and report is given to the receiving team.
